segments.text: For the Planning Bill and Natural Environments Bill,
Horizons Regional Council is joining us.
They've had a few technical, issues.
Uh, so we're going to run a little bit behind, this
afternoon. So, Horizons, welcome to the Select
Committee. Just in the essence of time, because we, we
don't have a lot of time, we will, hand it over to you to,
talk us through your submission, and then see if we have any time for
questions.
Kia ora, and thank you very much, Catherine, and appreci- thank you for,
squeezing us in literally. We've had some difficulty.
I'm Nicky Riley. I'm the chair of Horizons Regional Council,
and I'm just going to hand over very quickly to,
Fiona, my deputy, who will talk us through,
some of the finer points of our submission.
But, some of the key things we've leaned into was
the regulatory relief, freshwater farm plans and, the
goals. There'll be some things around the goals that may be a little different from
other pe- other submissions. Uh, but at a very high level, I'd
also like to say that we are leaning in and supporting of
the, bill and principle and the changes of direction that the
government are taking us with this.
So without further ado, I'll introduce you to my deputy, Fiona
Gordon.
Kia ora. Thank you, and, apologies for the
technical, issues that we've had.
Um, look, I'm just going to focus some of our comments at
this point. Uh, we're taking our submission as read, of
course, and, we're looking at focusing today's
commentary around the opportunities to improve the bills.
Uh, we are recommending that the legislation provide certainty
about the priorities and outcomes for managing air, water, coast and
land within environmental limits,
that it recognizes and retains environmental quality where it is above
ecological health and human health limits.
That is to avoid the risk of managing down, resulting in a reduction of the
quality of our environment. We're seeking that the bills
identify the goals as overarching outcomes that set the direction
and outcomes to be reached through decision-making.
Um, that is that all decision-making should be subject to the
goals. Including additional goals
is on our list of recommendations, and that is to provide for the
protection of vulnerable and threatened species and to recognize
enhancement of the environment. We note that Section 4 of the purpose of the
act is to establish a framework for the use, protection, and
enhancement of the natural environment.
We include recommendations around transition, transitional arrangements
to address the misalignment between the development of limits through national
environment plans and the risk that regional spatial plans are developed on
assumptions that later may prove inconsistent with the environmental
capacity. We also recommend additional provisions to
ensure uncertainty or a lack of information does not lead to conservative
assessments that delay or dilute responses to
emerging environmental pressures.
For example, we recommend that the yet-to-be-determined environmental
limit setting methodology should include a built-in
margin to provide for uncertainty, errors,
limited data, and climate change impacts.
We recommend that the bills recognize existing treaty
settlements, but also include the values and interests of
iwi, hapū and Māori that do not have settled
legislation. We seek that it provides a
system of regulatory relief that is affordable and
proportionate. We are concerned about the legal risk and
affordability of the proposed regulatory relief in the absence
of other funding sources. We ask
for a reconsideration of the resource use levies as these
have potential to increase the revenue taken from our
community. I would like to take a moment to highlight the goals and
our comments on these. We submit that the relationship between the two pieces
of legislation and the goals needs to be clear.
This will set a transparent and consistent framework in place that
leads the decisions and planning instruments that follow.
The goals should be overarching provisions, and all decisions made
under the legislation should be subject to the goals, as noted
earlier. This would assist with implementation by ensuring the
intended outcomes are recognized in national
direction, the combined plan, and are
consistent through the hierarchy and are clear and
predictable.
In terms of additional goals
that we believe are needed in the Natural Environment Plan, sorry, the
Natural Environment Bill, we seek to retain environmental
quality where it is above ecological health and human health limits
to provide for the protection of vulnerable species, vulnerable and threatened
species. For example, further clarity is required
around the goal no net loss of biodiversity.
What does this mean? And we would query how this goal
alone would meet the purpose of the act regarding protection and
enhancement. We also note that we need to recognize
enhancement
further. With regards to enhancement, as
a council, we believe that providing for non-regulatory methods
within the planning framework would recognize the critical
mechanism-- would recognize non-regulatory methods as a
critical mechanism for achieving enhancement of our natural
resources. Horizons is concerned that the regulatory relief
requirement will divert funds away from our existing
non-regulatory initiatives. The combination of regulation where
necessary and providing incentives to landowners in the form
of non-regulatory methods have certainly proven to be a successful
combination for delivering on enhancement of the natural
environment. Thank you.
Look, thank you very much for, that submission.
Look, we'll go straight to Lan Pham for a
question.
Tena koutou. Thanks so much for your submission.
I wanted to pick up on, the aspects of
biodiversity, particularly that you've just been mentioning.
And wondering if you'd expand, 'cause you've sort of
suggested that we actually recognize, like
IUCN threatened species and that kinda thing.
Do you think that's especially important if, for example, the
Wildlife Act override or provision,
is actually retained in the bill when the protective purpose
of the Wildlife Act won't have any effect in these
bills?
Um, I'm happy to answer that question. I'm, Yvonne Lagarde.
I work for Horizons. So the Wildlife Act, there's no
suggestion that in the legislation that those two will
override each other. So the purpose is very different in the
Wildlife Act. It's absolute protection, but the purpose in the
Natural Environment Bill is, use, protection and
enhancement. So, it may not override
it. Um, currently, the Wildlife Act, it's, there's a
separation between the species and the habitats.
So, where the Wildlife Act may look after species, it doesn't necessarily
retain habitats, and where a habitat is at risk, then species are
also at risk.
Tha- thank you. Um, look, I had a question, because we had Hastings
District Council earlier, talking about highly productive land
here, because a lot of our land is one and two.
And I know that in the Horowhenua area and around Horizons,
there is also a lot of highly productive
land. Um, and they in their submission, thought that the
spatial framework may, you know, lessen the protection of
that land. I didn't see that in your submission,
and you haven't obviously raised it.
I'm just wondering, how you feel about that and
whether, you see any threat to your highly productive
land.
Um, once again, that depends on how the Regional Spatial Plan is drafted and
the detail available. So at the moment, the current
system with, with LUC one, two, and three, it
does capture more land. Um, the level
of protection that it's offered is prescribed in the national direction, and we
don't know what that will say when it comes out, if it's amended or not.
So if it's LUC one and two, the
amount of land may be different. But circling back to the
information, there are many soil scientists that are not completely
comfortable with the level of information, so the LiDAR
i- isn't available for our whole region, for example.
So there will be places that are missed based on the information we have
available and the science we have available.
Okay, thank you. Um, Priyanka has a question.
Thank you. Um, mine is on regulatory relief, and
you've stated in your written submission that you're supportive of it.
We have heard from other, councils who have told us
that this would be, would place some pretty significant
financial pressures on them, particularly
coupled with some of the other changes that the current government's making,
such as the rate cap. Did you... I just wanted to give you an
opportunity to expand on what you have in your written submission and,
test whether this is a concern for you as well or not.
Do you want to answer that one?
Um, I will, give a a short intro, in
terms of answering that, and then pass over to the team.
Um, look, we do recognize that, there are
significant pieces of work that our landowners, for example, carry out.
Um, there are significant, you know, sometimes there are
significant, restrictions placed on landowners in terms of what they can
and can't do on their property because of various rules.
And, in certain cases, those will be for, you know, they're, they're about
public good. And so we see regulatory relief as being an
opportunity to reflect that. Um, but also we've made that comment
around, yeah, the rates capping, and the issues that it, it might impinge on
our ability to put funding towards those non-regulatory methods
that are focused more on enhancing, the environment.
And I'll, I'll pass over to the team for additional
comment.
Yeah, I don't really have anything to add there.
That, regulatory relief is one of a number of tools,
but it does seem to,
remove the opportunity under a rates capping environment from,
from the non-regulatory tools. So it, it's about really
what the policy settings are.
Look, thank you very much. It appears we have no further questions.
Um, thank you for your submission today.
Um, of course we have it, written there and we've, read it,
so thank you for that as well. Um, and yeah, we,
appreciate the technical difficulties, that you had,
so, you're not the only one. So thank you.
Thanks, Catherine. Thanks for making time and space for us today.
And just one final comment on the HPL.
Um, we do, value our highly productive land in class one and
two, and anything you can do to assist us with the situation in
Horowhenua would be greatly appreciated.
Yeah. No, thank you.
Right. We now move on to our next submitter, Te Kura
Takapini, who are there right in
front of us. Uh, welcome to the Select Committee.
Is it Gabrielle and Jess? Brilliant.
Look, you've got 15 minutes with us, so, we'll hand it over to you in the
essence of time to talk us through your submission, and then we'll open for some
questions.
Thank you. Um, my name's Gabrielle Huria, and I'm the Chief Executive
of Te Kura Takapini, the Ngāi Tahu Freshwater Group.
I also come from an old Ngāi Tahu fishing family
that practices the old ways of how we catch and
process our food source from the freshwater ways
of the South Island. Last week, the committee heard the broader Ngāi Tahu
submission. Today, we are s- focusing specifically on
freshwater. We made a separate freshwater s- submission
because the challenges facing freshwater in our takiwā are
significant, and the Natural Environment Bill will have major
implications for how those systems are managed in the
future. Today, we want to briefly highlight what we are
seeing on the ground in Te Waipounamu and
why getting this legislation right matters.
Two-thirds of New Zealand's surface water and seventy percent of its
groundwater are within our tribal territory.
What happens in the South Island shapes the national
outcome. But the system is under enormous
strain. Across our tribal territory, we see
over-allocation, contamination, ecosystem
modification, declining biodiversity, and drinking
water risks for our rural communities relying on private
wells. As an example, we have most of our marae
in rural areas. We have four marae currently that
have nitrates in their drinking water over the world
health standards. So it's not just us.
We test our wells regularly. It's all the private wells in the rural
areas in our takiwā. There's so many of them that have
nitrates over the world health standards that the people don't
realize. These pressures exist from the mountains to the
sea, and the status quo is not
sustainable. So as another example, Lake
Ellesmere or Te Waihora is one of the most polluted lakes in New
Zealand. We own the lake bed
of that lake, and so it has become a
giant filthy drain for all of Canterbury.
Recently, we tested the water in the lake, and it
was-- the, the air coming off the lake was
twenty-six times over the world health
standards. So that means if you go near the lake and you've
got asthma or you're elderly or you're a young child,
for health and safety purposes, you have to wear a face mask and
goggles so you don't breathe in the cyanobacteria.
So from our perspective, living like that along the lake, I used to gather
all of our eels and flounder from that lake.
I haven't done that for over a decade because it's too
dirty. It means we've entered Mad Max territory, where you
go out into the environment and you're wearing face mask and goggles not to be
poisoned by the environment. So I'll
s- I'll, I'll turn to Jess Riddell, who will speak further on the
issues.
So today we want to just highlight four key points from our
submission. The first one is around Māori freshwater rights and
interests.
From the Ngāi Tahu perspective, the Crown has repeatedly failed to honor its
commitments when it comes to Māori freshwater rights and interests,
and that failure is what led Ngāi Tahu to taking legal action against the
Crown on this, and we're currently still awaiting that judgment.
The issues remain unresolved, which is why we think that preservation clause in the
Natural Environment Bill is necessary.
But preservation is not progress.
And what we're asking for is that the Crown engage directly with Ngāi Tahu as a
priority. The Ngāi Tahu settlement, the active
litigation, and the scale of the freshwater challenges in this takiwā
mean that these issues cannot be simply deferred into a generic national
process. To be clear, the Ngāi Tahu litigation isn't
about water ownership or about a percentage allocation.
Ngāi Tahu are not seeking a share of the water, particularly in
over-allocated catchments where there is nothing left to
divide. Litigation is not the tribe's
objective. System redesign is.
Ngāi Tahu is already working with leading international researchers on
AI-enabled modeling, advanced monitoring tools, and improved
governance design. But the regulatory frameworks
are currently designed for last century and are constraining
innovation for the next. So if you want to see reform that
endures, this relationship between the Crown and Ngāi Tahu needs to
move beyond consultation and into genuine
co-design. The second point I want to touch on
is environmental limits. This is an area where co-design
is particularly important. Ngāi Tahu
supports strong binding limits that are
evidence-based, enforceable, robustly
monitored, and responsive to the evolving
science. The current NPS-FM bottom lines
are a practical starting point. However, we want to see in the
legislation a role for Ngāi Tahu which is
influential in setting limits at a national and regional
levels. We want those limits to be hard lines,
not aspirational targets. Cost and economic
considerations cannot be used to dilute those
ecological bottom lines. And Ngāi Tahu do not
support broad exceptions for significant infrastructure or
public benefit. If there are to be exceptions, these need
to be incredibly rare, tightly constrained, and
demonstra- demonstrably improve the environmental
outcomes. If limits are breached,
urgent and enforceable action must follow, including the
ability to review and adjust existing consents.
Without enforceable limits, reform will not restore ecological
health nor the public confidence.
Another brief example is the Manuherekea River in Central
Otago. It's currently over-allocated by eight times the
regional plan's take limit. By the time the river
joins another important waterway for us, the Matau, it has
been reduced to a trickle compared to its strong upper
reaches. Such low flows create serious ecological
risks, high water temperatures, loss of habitat for fish and
birds, higher nutrient concentrations, sediment build-up,
increased toxic... in-increased ri-risk of toxic
algal blooms. Ensuring appropriate environmental
limits, particularly minimum flow levels, is crucial for
the health of the Manuherekea and its mahinga kai.
And that's pretty much the same situation up the eastern
seaboard of Te Waipounamu.
The third point we really want to highlight today is around
policy implementation through digital solutions.
We know that resource management decisions are only as strong as the information
that sits behind them. But currently, the monitoring and consenting
systems are fragmented, inconsistent, and often still
manual. And these paper-based processes are, and
disconnected data sets are slowing consenting down,
weakening compliance, and undermining confidence in
decision-making. The 10-year review cycles,
that are for, regional plans are just too blunt for
a dynamic environmental system. And our
sub-submission proposes a shift towards real-time
monitoring, integrated visibility of consents,
particularly around, discharges and
allocation, scenario modeling and forecasting
tools, and clear triggers for adaptive
management. When we have transparent and accessible
information, that's what builds legitimacy.
Now, communities, iwi developers, and the regulators all
need this visibility to ensure there are clear
decision-making a-and visibility of what the trade-offs
are. And when that data is integrated and
visible, the trust and public confidence increases
too. This is particularly important as when,
the reform has a significant shift towards a more
permissive regime, so that's when monitoring and enforcement
become the frontline safeguard. If we're going to have more
permitted activities, that compliance becomes that environmental
backstop, and that backstop depends entirely
on strong data systems.
But investment in those data systems should not be seen as a cost.
It's risk mitigation infrastructure.
When we have poor data, we make poor decisions, and those
poor decisions are incredibly expensive to fix.
So if we wanna see durable reform, digital governance and s-
infrastructure must be embedded from the
outset. The last point we'd like to highlight
today is around resource levies.
Freshwater has long been treated as free and limitless,
and that's enabled some private game, gain while pushing the
contamination and restoration costs onto the communities and in
particular iwi. In effect, the public is
subsidizing polluters. And w- if we're
serious about enforceable limits, real-time monitoring, and
adaptive management, we must also be serious about how that system is
funded. That's why Ngāi Tahu supports a natural resource
levy system which will address over-allocation and
contamination, incentivize efficient and
high-value water use,
and also that money can be channeled into monitoring,
enforcement, and restoration. And we particularly support
if levies are ca- are collected in a particular catchment, that
money is sp- tagged for restoration and funding
into that same catchment so that the community can really see the
benefits of those levies.
The choice before this committee is to think about freshwater
reform will define New Zealand's environmental
credibility and economic resilience for generations.
It's not hydro or dairy or farming that are the
backbone of the New Zealand economy, it's fresh water.
The question is not whether change will occur, it is whether change will be
shaped deliberately or imposed by ecological
failure. Ngāi Tahu is not here simply to
critique. We want to partner in the decision, design, and
delivery of a system that integrates
rights for everyone clearly, enforceable limits,
modern data infrastructure, and economic
discipline. We want to help build a freshwater
system our grandchildren can inherit with
confidence, not one they are forced to
repair. We welcome your questions. Ngā mihi.
Thank you very much for that submission.
A lot in there and appreciate your time on it.
Uh, look, we'll go to a question from Lan Pham.
E nga koroa, thank you so much for your very comprehensive
submission, and there's so much to get into with it.
But I wanted to start with this first question, and that was
around, your request
that the committee actually includes an express
ability to review and adjust conditions of existing
permits in a catchment where limits are reached or
exceeded. And I note that later in your submission you give
the example of Ōtūwharekai, and I'm
interested in sort of what the c-committee might be able to learn from
that experience, and then how you
view
The parts of the bill that
basically say councils have to be satisfied that voluntary
measures will not meet the environmental limits before they
can take a regulatory approach.
Keen on any thoughts there.
No, thanks. Thank you for the question, Lan.
I think, Otawhirikai is an incredibly interesting example
because what the situation there is that
all the farmers in that catchment are actually doing
everything within their consent and within the planning limits,
yet there's an in-- there's an incredibly,
um... We've seen a deterioration in the water
quality there. So we are seeing a
situation where everything is legal and above board, but yet we're
not achieving the desired environmental outcomes.
So there's something wrong in that system there.
And even though, the farmers are doing a great efforts when it comes to
farm planning and achieving A grades and doing all they
can, but it's not working enough. So that's when
we need to have, tools there in the kitty to be able
to adjust consents, adjust the planning rules,
not wait for a plan change or a ten-year planning review or
the end of a thirty-five-year consent.
The environment simply does not have time to
wait for these arbitrary processes that we've put in
place in legislation in a planning world.
That's why we're asking for a system that is more
dynamic and can a-- we have clear triggers set out that
when we hit this point, we're not seeing the outcomes we want, then we
need to do something different.
Look, thank you very much. Look, we have met time.
Uh, we appreciate your time today, but we are gonna have to wrap it up there.
But, yeah, a great submission. So thank you very much.
Thank you. Thank you. Ka kite.
So we now move to our next submitter, which is Chorus
New Zealand.
Good afternoon, Andrew. We can see you there.
Welcome to the Select Committee this afternoon.
Look, we will hand it over to you to talk us through your submission, and then
we'll open up the questions. Thank you.
Okay. Good afternoon, and, thank you for the opportunity to speak today.
I'm Andrew Cantor, the Environmental Planning Manager here at Chorus, New
Zealand's largest telecommunications infrastructure provider.
Our network supports more than one point one million broadband connections and is
embedded in every region, district, and territorial jurisdiction in the
country. Because we operate everywhere, we are acutely aware
of how a fragmented and inconsistent planning system can slow down
infrastructure delivery, increase costs, and create uncertainty
for both providers and communities.
For these reasons, Chorus strongly supports the intent of the reform, particularly
the shift toward greater national consistency,
and a system that is clearer, more predictable, and more enabling for essential
infrastructure. Telecommunications are now
essential infrastructure. Every bit as critical as electricity, water, and
transport. Our network underpins economic growth, education,
social connection, healthcare, and community resilience.
We saw this vividly during COVID-19.
When New Zealand shut down physically, it stayed open
digital-digitally. Fiber kept households connected, kept
businesses operating, and is still just as essential today in maintaining
connectivity, for example, holding Select Committee hearings via
Zoom. So as New Zealand embarks on major reform of its
resource management system, it's crucial that telecommunications infrastructure,
particularly fiber, is recognized, enabled, and planned for in a
clear and consistent way across the country.
Our written submission covered a wide range of issues from the interaction between
the two bills, the management of effects, emergency works, and
designations. At a high level, our position is that reforms are heading
in the right direction. They present a real opportunity for a more enabling
and consistent system. And with targeted refinements, they can be
made clearer, more efficient, and more supportive of essential infrastructure.
Today, I'll focus on four key points that are particularly important to Chorus
and to New Zealand's digital connectivity.
The first is in regard to the new permitted activity registration system.
Chorus undertakes more than five thousand permitted activities a year.
Straightforward, low impact work like customer connections and routine
maintenance, predominantly controlled under the National Environmental Standard
for Telecommunications Facilities.
The bills w-would require these activities to be registered, assessed, and
monitored by councils. That alone could create thousands of minor
administrative processes annually without providing any corresponding
environmental benefit. For inf-infrastructure providers and for
councils, this would introduce cost, delay, and uncertainty where none
currently exists. Our request is simple, to remove the
registration requirement altogether, or at a minimum, exclude activities
permitted by national standards or those undertaken by critical
infrastructure providers.
The second p-point we would like to focus on is in regard to infrastructure
goals. Under the new system, statutory goals guide every
planning and consenting decision.
That means the wording of the infrastructure goal is
vital. Planning Bill-- In the Planning Bill, infrastructure
goal is drafted more weakly than others.
It asks for decision makers to plan and provide for infrastructure, while other
goals require them to protect, ensure, support, or
enable. That creates an unnecessary risk that infrastructure gets
deprioritized in the balancing exercise.
In the National Environment Bill, there is no infrastructure goal at all, despite
the NEB shaping national instruments and consenting pathways.
To avoid inconsistent outcomes and provide for integrated management, we need
stronger, clearer goals. We recommend strengthening the Planning Bill, so it
requires decision makers to protect, enable, and support
infrastructure.Recognising public benefit and operational constraints
and inserting an equivalent infrastructure goal into the National Environment Bill.
Third point I'd like to focus on is the development of national
instruments. National instruments will shape the entire system, and they need
to work effectively. At present, the time frames are short,
there's no provision for hearings or involvement of key stakeholders, and
without safeguards, new national standards could inadvertently
backslide from the recently reviewed and updated national standards,
such as the National Environmental Standard for Telecommunications Facilities.
Given how central these are to the new system, we recommend
ensuring national standards are no less enabling than existing RMA
national direction,
extending development time frames from nine to twelve months, and
enabling key stakeholder engagement and hearings during their development.
This is essential to ensure good, durable national rules for
infrastructure. The final point I'd like to focus on
is the need to establish a national standard for telecommunications
servicing in new developments. This is Chorus's highest
priority. New Zealand has invested heavily in world-class open
access fibre networks. Yet despite that, a growing number of new
subdivisions are being built without any fibre at all, or with
private closed fibre systems that lock residents into a single
provider. Our experience is that these cases are
increasing, particularly in areas where district plans are outdated
and lack clear requirements for telecommunications servicing.
Many subdivision rules haven't been updated in a decade or more, meaning
developers can omit fibre entirely or install proprietary networks
without consumers understanding the implications.
The impacts are significant. Homeowners assume fibre will be
available, only to discover after purchase that it isn't, or that
retrofitting require, will require disruptive, expensive civil works across
multiple properties. Where private networks are installed, people are
locked into limited provider choice, sometimes just a single retailer
with restricted plans and often lower performance.
These decisions undermine digital equity, reduce
competition, and erode the public investment made through the UFB
programme. The upcoming reforms provide a once in a generation
opportunity to fix this. We already require water, power, and
roading infrastructure in subdivisions.
It is entirely appropriate to expect modern digital infrastructure to be treated
the same way. Internationally, this is standard practice.
For example, Australia requires developers to install modern fibre-ready
infrastructure in new developments, ensuring consumer choice and competition
from day one. Our recommendation is clear: new national
standards should establish a default requirement for open access fibre in all
new residential and commercial developments where it is reasonably
practicable. These, this requirement should apply consistently
nationwide, allow flexibility where fibre genuinely cannot be
installed, and define open access in line with the
Telecommunications Act to ensure networks are wholesale, neutral,
and support full retail competition.
This single change would prevent digital inequity in new communities,
ensure that public investment in UFB is not undermined by fragmented
private networks, give developers clarity and certainty, and
future-proof New Zealand's digital infrastructure for decades to come.
For Chorus, this is the most important issue before the committee today.
To close, Chorus strongly supports the intent of these reforms.
They can create a clearer, more enabling, and more consistent system that
recognises the essential nature of infrastructure and supports New Zealand's
long-term wellbeing and economic growth.
The targeted amendments we've outlined, particularly the establishment of a
national open access fibre requirement, the bills can ensure every new community is
built with the digital foundations it needs to thrive.
Thank you for your time. Happy to take questions.
Look, thank you, Andrew. Uh, some really interesting points that you've made there
in the submission. Uh, look, I'll go to Ryan Hamilton for a question
first.
Thanks. Um, just picking up your point around emergency,
provisions. Uh, obviously extending from thirty
days to sixty days makes sense. I was just trying to wrap my h-head around the
seven to fourteen days.
Um, how does that work? 'Cause y-obviously you wanted, to, to
provide emergency notification, I thought you'd want less days, but I've probably
got that round back to front.
Um,
would that be... Sorry, I'm just trying to get to that page.
It's on, page seventeen of your submission.
Um, just written emergency
works.
Uh, so that would enable us to have more time to allow
to, to notify the consenting authority, being the
council-
... typically, that those works have taken place, is my understanding.
Yeah. So it's pretty straightforward.
It's just a bit compressed when you're obviously under a lot of pressure in those
environments.
That's correct. And, and pulling those applications together or those
notifications together requires a lot of sort of technical input, and
those people are sort of entirely consumed in getting the network up
and running in those situations.
Yeah. Yeah. Sure. Thank you.
Um, look, I just wanna ask a quick question because, you t-said that
your main priority was to create the national standard for open
access fibre. Um, and you talked about some other
jurisdictions, you mentioned Australia.
But, a-are you seeing this as quite broad practice
across, other international jurisdictions around the
world? I mean, you've just used Australia as an example, but is that, is that
common practice?
Sure. So Australia, as I mentioned, they've got a, a sort of national policy on
telecommunications and new developments.
Uh, in the UK and parts of Europe, they have
similar requirements. They might be technology agnostic, but
require a minimum sort of speed. I think they call it gigabit
ready. Um, so that could sort of
allow, I assume, alternative types of technology, although
The benefit of fibre is once it's in the ground, it's sort of that ongoing
scalability and performance upgrades are very quick and simple.
You just tweak the electronics at either end.
Yeah. And obviously this is from the national direction point of view-
Yeah
... like creating the national standard.
But, like, how you-- the, I can't see here that you've, you've,
you've just kind of talked about it, but what, what, what would that actually look
like-
Uh, I guess-
